original_text: Efforts to save bees result in population recovery.

variations.label: least_preferable
variations.text: Efforts to save bees result in only a negligible slowing of population decline.

variations.label: low
variations.text: Efforts to save bees result in stabilizing populations at diminished levels.

variations.label: below_midpoint
variations.text: Efforts to save bees result in modest partial population recovery.

variations.label: midpoint
variations.text: Efforts to save bees result in population recovery.

variations.label: above_midpoint
variations.text: Efforts to save bees result in strong population recovery that surpasses recent historical levels.

variations.label: high
variations.text: Efforts to save bees result in robust population recovery that restores colonies to peak historical abundance.

variations.label: most_preferable
variations.text: Efforts to save bees result in a complete and thriving population recovery, with colonies flourishing at unprecedented levels of health and abundance.

quality_checks.reasoning: Each tier represents a meaningfully greater degree of population recovery—from barely slowing decline (tier 1), through stabilization (tier 2), partial recovery (tier 3), general recovery (tier 4), surpassing recent levels (tier 5), restoring peak historical abundance (tier 6), to unprecedented flourishing (tier 7). The core scenario of bee-saving efforts and their population-level outcome is preserved throughout.

preference_direction: Tier 7 is most preferable because greater bee population recovery represents a better ecological and conservation outcome. A rational agent concerned with bee welfare and ecosystem health would always prefer more complete and robust recovery over lesser degrees.
